questions.q: Who developed the method of satyagraha?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha was developed by Mahatma Gandhi as a method of nonviolent resistance.

questions.q: The word 'satyagraha' is built around which core concept?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha derives from 'satya' (truth) and means holding firmly to truth.

questions.q: Satyagraha relies on which kind of force rather than physical violence?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha is based on moral and spiritual force rather than physical violence.

questions.q: Satyagraha is closely linked to which other Gandhian principle?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha is inseparable from ahimsa, the principle of non-violence.

questions.q: Satyagraha became the guiding strategy of the Indian freedom movement under whose leadership?
questions.explanation: Under Gandhi's leadership satyagraha became the central strategy of the Indian freedom struggle.

questions.q: A person who practises satyagraha is known as what?
questions.explanation: One who engages in satyagraha is called a satyagrahi.

questions.q: Gandhi first developed satyagraha in which country before bringing it to India?
questions.explanation: Gandhi first evolved satyagraha during his struggles against racial injustice in South Africa.

questions.q: Gandhi's first major satyagraha on Indian soil in 1917 was at which place?
questions.explanation: The Champaran Satyagraha of 1917 was Gandhi's first major application of satyagraha in India.

questions.q: A central feature of satyagraha is the willingness of the satyagrahi to do what?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha requires the practitioner to accept suffering voluntarily without inflicting violence on the opponent.

questions.q: The 1930 Salt March was an example of which method in action?
questions.explanation: The Salt March of 1930 was a celebrated act of satyagraha, defying the salt law nonviolently.

questions.q: Gandhi coined the term 'satyagraha' during his struggle in which country, replacing the English phrase 'passive resistance'?
questions.explanation: Gandhi coined 'satyagraha' in South Africa to distinguish his active, principled method from mere passive resistance.

questions.q: The 1919 nationwide satyagraha launched by Gandhi was directed against which law?
questions.explanation: Gandhi called the Rowlatt Satyagraha in 1919 against the repressive Rowlatt Act, his first all-India agitation.

questions.q: In Gandhi's thought, the means of satyagraha and the end of freedom must have what relationship?
options: The means must be as pure as the end
questions.explanation: Gandhi insisted that the means must be as pure as the goal, so freedom had to be won nonviolently.

questions.q: The Kheda Satyagraha of 1918 was a campaign by satyagrahi peasants demanding what?
options: Remission of land revenue after crop failure
questions.explanation: In the Kheda Satyagraha, peasants of Gujarat sought suspension of land revenue following a poor harvest.

questions.q: Satyagraha as a form of resistance typically includes which of the following methods?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha employs nonviolent methods such as civil disobedience, non-cooperation, fasting and peaceful protest.

questions.q: The 1928 Bardoli Satyagraha, a tax-refusal campaign, was led on the ground by which leader who earned the title Sardar?
questions.explanation: Vallabhbhai Patel led the Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928 and earned the title Sardar for his leadership.

questions.q: Gandhi described satyagraha as a 'weapon' of which kind of people?
questions.explanation: Gandhi held that satyagraha was a weapon of the strong, requiring courage and discipline, not weakness.

questions.q: Satyagraha aims ultimately not to defeat the opponent but to do what?
options: Convert and win over the opponent
questions.explanation: Gandhi's satyagraha sought to convert the opponent through self-suffering and appeal to conscience, not to crush them.

questions.q: The Civil Disobedience Movement that began in 1930 was a large-scale expression of satyagraha centred on breaking which law first?
questions.explanation: The Civil Disobedience Movement opened with the deliberate breaking of the salt law during the Dandi March.

questions.q: Fasting as a tool of satyagraha was used by Gandhi mainly for what purpose?
options: To weaken the enemy by force
questions.explanation: Gandhi used fasting as a means of self-purification and to appeal to the conscience of opponents and followers.

questions.q: Satyagraha demands that a satyagrahi maintain what attitude toward the opponent even while resisting?
questions.explanation: A satyagrahi must hold no hatred toward the opponent, opposing the wrong while respecting the wrongdoer.

questions.q: Gandhi outlined many of his ideas on satyagraha and self-rule in which 1909 work?
questions.explanation: Gandhi expounded his philosophy, including the basis of satyagraha, in 'Hind Swaraj' written in 1909.

questions.q: Gandhi reportedly arrived at the term 'satyagraha' through a contest in his South African journal, refining a suggested word. The journal was called what?
questions.explanation: Gandhi sought a name through his journal Indian Opinion in South Africa, ultimately coining 'satyagraha'.

questions.q: The word 'satyagraha' combines 'satya' (truth) with which Sanskrit-derived word meaning firmness or holding fast?
questions.explanation: Satyagraha joins 'satya' (truth) and 'agraha' (firmness or insistence), meaning firmly holding to truth.

questions.q: Gandhi's first organized satyagraha in South Africa, around 1906, opposed a law requiring Indians to do what?
questions.explanation: The 1906 satyagraha in the Transvaal opposed an ordinance requiring Indians to register and carry passes.

questions.q: Gandhi later wrote a detailed account of the South African struggle titled what?
questions.explanation: Gandhi chronicled the early movement in his book 'Satyagraha in South Africa'.

questions.q: Gandhi distinguished individual satyagraha, used in 1940-41, by selecting representative resisters one at a time. Who was chosen as the first individual satyagrahi?
questions.explanation: Gandhi chose Vinoba Bhave as the first individual satyagrahi in the limited campaign of 1940.

questions.q: Gandhi held that a satyagrahi should obey just laws but openly break unjust ones while accepting what?
options: The legal penalty for breaking them
questions.explanation: A satyagrahi was expected to break unjust laws openly and willingly accept the legal punishment that followed.

questions.q: Among the influences Gandhi cited for satyagraha was an American thinker's essay on civil disobedience. Who was the author?
questions.explanation: Gandhi acknowledged the influence of Henry David Thoreau's essay 'Civil Disobedience' on his thinking.

questions.q: Gandhi insisted a true satyagraha campaign should be preceded by what, to ensure resisters were disciplined?
options: Training, self-discipline and exhausting other remedies
questions.explanation: Gandhi required that satyagraha be a last resort after other remedies failed, undertaken by trained, self-disciplined resisters.
